title: Dog Coughing Up Blood: Emergency Guide to Hemoptysis
meta_desc: Seeing your dog cough up blood is frightening. Understand the causes of hemoptysis and why immediate veterinary care is essential for your pet.
content_html:
TL;DR: Coughing up blood (hemoptysis) is a high-priority veterinary emergency that requires immediate medical attention to address potentially life-threatening causes like heart failure or poisoning.

What is hemoptysis and what does it look like in dogs?

Hemoptysis is the medical term for coughing up blood from the respiratory tract. It occurs when blood vessels in the lungs, trachea, or airways rupture and leak into the air passages. Common signs include:

  • Bright red streaks in phlegm
  • Pink frothy discharge
  • Significant blood clots
  • Distinguishable from hematemesis (vomiting blood from the stomach)

Is coughing up blood a veterinary emergency for my dog?

Yes. The urgency level for hemoptysis is High. If your dog is coughing up blood, you should transport your pet to the nearest emergency veterinary hospital immediately, as it can indicate life-threatening conditions such as:

  • Internal trauma
  • Severe pneumonia
  • Heart failure
  • Exposure to toxic substances like rat poison

How does taking a photo of the blood help with veterinary triage?

If your dog is stable enough for a quick snapshot, taking a clear photo of the blood or discharge can provide the veterinary team with essential diagnostic clues, including:

  • The color and consistency of the blood (bright red and frothy vs. dark and clotted)
  • Whether the blood is mixed with mucus
  • Information that helps the vet determine the source and severity of the bleeding before the physical exam begins

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, heartworm disease may lead to pulmonary arterial thromboembolism that commonly results in dyspnea and tachypnea; affected animals are often reportedly healthy until the sudden onset of coughing, hemoptysis, respiratory distress, or sudden death. Pulmonary thromboembolism may also develop from spontaneous worm death or pulmonary thrombosis secondary to pulmonary endothelial damage. Clinical signs are nonspecific and can range from mild to profound, with dyspnea, tachypnea, depression, cyanosis, collapse, and shock also possible. Thoracic radiographs may be normal or show underperfusion of the affected lung lobe, interstitial to alveolar infiltrates, or pleural effusion. Pulmonary thromboembolism in dogs is associated with protein-losing nephropathy, heartworm disease, endocarditis, cardiomyopathy, necrotizing pancreatitis, hypercortisolism, immune-mediated hemolytic anemia, sepsis, diabetes mellitus, neoplasia, atherosclerosis, trauma, and major surgical procedures.

Chapter: Cardiology, Pulmonology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 143)

faqs.question: What are common causes of a dog coughing up blood?
faqs.answer: Potential causes include severe heartworm disease, pulmonary edema, lung tumors, physical trauma to the chest, or the ingestion of anticoagulant rodenticides (rat poison).

faqs.question: Can I wait until morning to see my regular vet?
faqs.answer: No. Because coughing up blood can lead to sudden respiratory failure or internal shock, your dog needs to be evaluated by an emergency veterinarian immediately, regardless of the time of day.

faqs.question: What should I do while transporting my dog to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog as calm and quiet as possible. Minimize their movement to prevent an increased heart rate, which could worsen the bleeding, and ensure they have plenty of cool air for breathing.
